questions.solution: S

questions.solution: Cannot be determined

questions.solution: Both follow

questions.stem: Statements: All squares are rectangles. All rectangles are parallelograms. No parallelogram is a triangle. Conclusions: I. No square is a triangle. II. Some rectangles are not triangles.
questions.solution: Both follow

questions.stem: Statement: The passing percentage in the exam was 85%, the highest in a decade. Conclusion I: The exam was easier this year. II: Students worked harder this year.
questions.solution: Neither follows

questions.stem: If PANEL = 50, BOARD = 45, CHAIR = 38, then FLOOR = ?

questions.solution: Circle

questions.stem: If MASTER = 444, STUDENT = 606, LEARN = ?

questions.stem: A is the brother of B. B is the sister of C. C is the father of D. How is A related to D?
questions.solution: Uncle

questions.stem: Find the next: 1, 8, 27, 64, 125, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: ____ the rain, the match was cancelled.
questions.solution: Owing to

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: Neither he nor his brother are going to the party.
options: Neither he nor his brother were going to the party.
Neither he nor his brother are going to the party.
Neither he nor his brother have been going to the party.
Neither he nor his brother is going to the party.
questions.solution: Neither he nor his brother is going to the party.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She is married ____ a mining engineer.
questions.solution: to

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: I have been working here from 2015.
options: I have been working here since 2015.
I have been working here from the year 2015.
I have been working here from 2015.
questions.solution: I have been working here since 2015.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The company is committed ____ improving safety.
questions.solution: to

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: One of the miners have been hospitalised.
options: One of the miners has been hospitalised.
One of the miners is being hospitalised.
One of the miners are hospitalised.
One of the miners were hospitalised.
questions.solution: One of the miners has been hospitalised.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: ____ the circumstances, we decided to postpone.
questions.solution: Under

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He is one of those who has excelled in the field.
options: He is one of those who have excelled in the field.
He is one who has excelled in the field.
He is one of those who excelled in the field.
He is one of those who has excelled in the field.
questions.solution: He is one of those who have excelled in the field.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She was accused ____ stealing company data.
questions.solution: of

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She is the tallest of the two sisters.
options: She is the taller of the two sisters.
She is the tallest of the two sisters.
She is more tall of the two sisters.
She is tall among the two sisters.
questions.solution: She is the taller of the two sisters.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: No sooner did he arrive ____ the meeting began.
questions.solution: than

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: I prefer coffee than tea.
questions.solution: I prefer coffee to tea.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Hardly had we left ____ it started raining.
questions.solution: when

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: Hardly had he arrived than the meeting started.
options: He had hardly arrived than the meeting started.
Hardly had he arrived then the meeting started.
Hardly he had arrived than the meeting started.
Hardly had he arrived when the meeting started.
questions.solution: Hardly had he arrived when the meeting started.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She was so tired ____ she fell asleep immediately.
questions.solution: that

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: The number of accidents have increased.
options: A number of accidents has increased.
The number of accidents has increased.
The number of accidents is increasing.
The number of accidents have increased.
questions.solution: The number of accidents has increased.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The company faced losses ____ poor management decisions.
questions.solution: due to

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: workers / the / demanded / conditions / working / better
options: Demanded the workers better working conditions
Working conditions demanded the workers better
The workers demanded better working conditions
The workers demanded better working conditions
Better working conditions the workers demanded
questions.solution: The workers demanded better working conditions

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The government is ____ to attract investment.
questions.solution: seeking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of INNOCUOUS:
questions.solution: Harmful

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To go the extra mile':
questions.solution: To make extra effort

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of MUNDANE:
questions.solution: Extraordinary

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To cost an arm and a leg':
questions.solution: To be very expensive

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of HARMONY:
questions.solution: Discord

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of COMPLACENT:
questions.solution: Self-satisfied

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The STL contains:
options: Containers, algorithms, iterators, and function objects
questions.solution: STL: vector, list, map, set (containers) + sort, find, for_each (algorithms) + iterators.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In paged segmentation, each segment has:
questions.solution: Segmentation + paging: each segment subdivided into pages with its own PT.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache associativity types:
questions.solution: Direct: each block → 1 location. Full: any location. Set: n-way (compromise).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Greibach Normal Form (GNF):
options: All productions are A → aα (terminal followed by zero or more non-terminals)
questions.solution: GNF: A → aB₁B₂...Bₖ (single terminal + non-terminals). Useful for parsing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'final' keyword in Java for a variable means:
questions.solution: final variable = const. final class = cannot be inherited. final method = cannot be overridden.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The pipe operator '|' in Unix:
options: Connects stdout of one command to stdin of another
questions.solution: cmd1 | cmd2: output of cmd1 → input of cmd2.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 2K memory locations?
questions.solution: 2K = 2048 = 2^11, so 11 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The regular expression for strings starting with 'a' and ending with 'b' is:
questions.solution: a(a+b)*b: start with a, any middle (a or b), end with b.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Function pointers in C:
options: Store the address of a function (can be called through the pointer)
questions.solution: Function pointer: int (*fp)(int, int). Useful for callbacks and dynamic dispatch.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SCAN disk scheduling algorithm:
options: Moves the head from one end to the other, servicing requests along the way
questions.solution: SCAN (elevator) = move in one direction, service, then reverse.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The parity bit is used for:
questions.solution: Parity = single-bit error detection (odd or even parity).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Alpha conversion in lambda calculus:
questions.solution: α-conversion: λx.x = λy.y (bound variable renaming). Avoids variable capture during substitution.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A friend function in C++:
options: Can access private and protected members of the class (not a member itself)
Is a member of the class
Cannot be defined outside the class
questions.solution: friend: non-member function granted access to private members. Breaks encapsulation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The size of virtual address space is limited by:
options: Number of bits in the address (e.g., 2^64)
questions.solution: Virtual address space = 2^(address bits), independent of RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.9. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem that is NP-complete is:
options: In NP and at least as hard as every NP problem
Solvable in polynomial time for sure
questions.solution: NP-complete = in NP and NP-hard; a polynomial solution to one would solve all of NP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A template in C++:
options: Can only be used with classes
questions.solution: Template: compile-time code generation. Function templates and class templates.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The context switch involves:
options: Saving and restoring the state of a process/thread
questions.solution: Context switch: save PCB of current process, load PCB of next process.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.95. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The subset construction converts:
options: NFA to DFA (states = subsets of NFA states)
questions.solution: Subset construction: NFA with n states → DFA with up to 2^n states (exponential blowup).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A constructor in C++ is:
options: A function that destroys the object
A special member function called when an object is created
questions.solution: Constructor initializes objects. Destructor cleans up when object is destroyed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A system call is:
options: An interface between user programs and the OS kernel
questions.solution: System call: user mode → kernel mode (e.g., read(), fork(), exec()).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 6-stage pipeline processes 1000 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The complement of a recursive language is:
questions.solution: Recursive languages are closed under complement (just flip accept/reject).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dynamic memory allocation in C using malloc:
options: Returns void* to allocated memory (or NULL on failure)
questions.solution: malloc(size): returns void* (cast to desired type). calloc(n, size): zero-initialized. realloc(ptr, new_size).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm is used for deadlock:
questions.solution: It checks whether granting a request leaves the system in a safe state (avoidance).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 6-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A universal Turing machine (UTM):
options: Is more powerful than a regular TM
Can simulate any other Turing machine (takes TM description as input)
questions.solution: UTM: reads (M, w) and simulates M on w. Proves existence of universal computing devices.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Consteval in C++20:
options: Forces compile-time evaluation (unlike constexpr which may be evaluated at runtime)
questions.solution: consteval: immediate functions that must produce a constant expression. Stricter than constexpr.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A reader-writer lock allows:
options: Multiple concurrent readers or exclusive writer access
questions.solution: RW lock: many readers OR one writer (optimized for read-heavy workloads).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A benchmark measures:
questions.solution: Benchmarks: SPEC (CPU), LINPACK (HPC), Geekbench (mixed), dhrystone/whetstone (classic).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A finite automaton has:
options: A finite set of states, input alphabet, transition function, start state, and accepting states
questions.solution: FA: 5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F). DFA = deterministic, NFA = non-deterministic.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Coroutines in C++20:
options: Functions that can suspend execution and resume later (co_await, co_yield, co_return)
questions.solution: Coroutines: stackless (heap-allocated frame). co_await suspends, co_yield returns value. co_return completes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'wait()' system call:
options: Makes parent wait for child termination
questions.solution: wait(): parent blocks until child exits (collects exit status).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Moore's law observed:
options: Transistor density doubles approximately every 2 years
questions.solution: Moore's law (1965): transistor count on IC doubles ~2 years. Slowing since ~2010 (end of Dennard scaling).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Rice's theorem states:
options: Any non-trivial property of the language of a TM is undecidable
All properties of TMs are decidable
questions.solution: Rice: semantic properties of RE languages are undecidable (e.g., emptiness, finiteness, regularity).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The heap is used for:
questions.solution: Heap: manually managed (malloc/free, new/delete). Stack: automatic (function frames).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging eliminates:
questions.solution: Paging eliminates external fragmentation. May have internal (last page).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 4-stage pipeline processes 100 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The complement of a recursively enumerable language:
options: May not be RE (if neither L nor complement are RE, L is not recursive)
questions.solution: A language is recursive iff both L and its complement are RE.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Signal handling in C:
options: Registers a function (signal handler) to be called when a signal is received
questions.solution: signal(SIGINT, handler): handler must be async-signal-safe (can't call printf safely).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The readers-writers problem prioritizes:
options: Either readers or writers (depends on solution)
questions.solution: First readers-writers: readers priority. Second: writers priority.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DMA (Direct Memory Access) allows:
options: I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention
questions.solution: DMA controller handles data transfer, freeing CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem is NP-complete if:
options: It is in NP and every NP problem is reducible to it in polynomial time
questions.solution: NP-complete = NP-hard + in NP. First: SAT (Cook-Levin theorem).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Template specialization:
options: Provides a custom implementation for a specific template parameter type
questions.solution: Explicit specialization: template<> class MyClass<int> { ... }. Partial specialization for classes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Microkernel has:
options: Minimal kernel with services in user space (e.g., Minix, QNX)
questions.solution: Microkernel: IPC-based communication between user-space servers. More reliable.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The program counter (PC) holds:
options: The address of the next instruction to be executed
questions.solution: PC = instruction pointer (IP in x86). Incremented after fetch (or modified by jumps).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The CYK algorithm:
options: Parses a string in O(n) time
Decides membership of a string in a CFG in O(n³) time
questions.solution: CYK = Cocke-Younger-Kasami. Dynamic programming for CFG membership (requires CNF).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The stack grows:
questions.solution: Stack grows downward. Heap grows upward. Growth direction = architecture-dependent (typically down).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: LRU approximation uses:
options: Reference bits in page table entries (periodically cleared by OS)
questions.solution: LRU exact is expensive. Clock algorithm uses reference bit (second chance).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 4-stage pipeline processes 1000 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every NFA can be converted to:
options: A Turing machine with more tapes
questions.solution: NFA → DFA subset construction: O(2^n) states worst case.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The null character in C is:
questions.solution: Null terminator '\0' marks end of C strings. NULL = (void*)0 for pointers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dining philosophers problem illustrates:
questions.solution: Classic synchronization problem demonstrating deadlock and starvation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ALU performs:
questions.solution: ALU = arithmetic (add/sub/mul/div) + logic (AND/OR/XOR/NOT).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For every nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA) there exists:
questions.solution: NFAs and DFAs recognize exactly the same (regular) languages — the subset construction proves it.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The restrict keyword in C:
options: Tells the compiler that a pointer is the only way to access the object (enables optimization)
questions.solution: restrict: no aliasing through this pointer. Compiler can optimize without worrying about overlaps.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of the naive string matching algorithm is:
questions.solution: Naive string matching = O(m×n) worst case.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 11001000 equals:
questions.solution: MSB = 1 ⇒ negative; value = 200 − 256 = -56.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 8, push 12, pop, push 30. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 30 leaves 30 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">30</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The isolation level SNAPSHOT:
options: Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot of the database at its start time
questions.solution: Snapshot isolation: uses MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control). No dirty/non-repeatable/phantom reads.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Floyd-Warshall algorithm:
options: Solves all-pairs shortest paths in O(V³) using DP
questions.solution: Floyd-Warshall: DP over intermediate vertices. Works with negative edges (no negative cycles).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An encoder converts:
questions.solution: Encoder: 2^n→n (e.g., 8-to-3 encoder). Priority encoder resolves conflicts.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The octal representation of decimal 31 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Gray code has the property that:
options: Successive values differ by exactly one bit
questions.solution: Gray code = unit-distance code (adjacent differ by 1 bit).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Heapify (building a heap from array) complexity:
questions.solution: Building heap: sift down from n/2 to 1. Sum of heights = O(n). Top-down insertion = O(n log n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore is primarily used for:
questions.solution: Semaphores coordinate access to shared resources, preventing race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of binary search for n=8 is:
questions.solution: Binary search halves the search space each step: O(log n) ≈ O(log 8).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A switch (LAN) forwards frames using:
options: MAC addresses at the data-link layer
IP addresses at the network layer
Port numbers at the transport layer
questions.solution: A Layer-2 switch learns and forwards by MAC address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'du' command shows:
options: Disk usage (size of files and directories)
questions.solution: du -sh *: summary of each item. du -h --max-depth=1. ncdu: interactive.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The PCB (Process Control Block) contains:
options: Process state, registers, priority, memory info, open files
questions.solution: PCB = kernel data structure for each process (task_struct in Linux).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An interrupt vector is:
options: An array of pointers to ISRs (indexed by interrupt number)
questions.solution: Interrupt vector table (IVT): maps interrupt numbers to handler addresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer) is:
options: A hardware cache for page table entries
questions.solution: TLB = fast associative cache for recently used page table entries.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 100 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 100/100 = 1 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 200 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:
questions.solution: time = size/rate = 200/250 = 0.8 s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In round-robin CPU scheduling, each process is given:
options: A fixed time quantum in turn
questions.solution: Round-robin cycles through ready processes, each running for one time quantum.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TCP's flow control uses:
questions.solution: Flow control = receiver window (rwnd). Congestion control = congestion window (cwnd).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The average and worst-case time complexity of merge sort is:
questions.solution: Merge sort always divides and merges in O(n log n), and it is stable.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Segmentation divides memory into:
options: Variable-sized logical segments (code, data, stack, heap)
questions.solution: Segment: logical unit (code segment, data segment). External fragmentation issue.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 6 elements is:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes n(n−1)/2 = 6×5/2 = 15 comparisons.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time complexity of finding an element in a skiplist is:
questions.solution: Skiplist: expected O(log n) search time with balanced levels.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 11, push 20, pop, push 30. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 30 leaves 30 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">30</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">11</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Story points in Agile:
options: A relative unit of effort estimation (complexity, risk, and effort combined)
questions.solution: Story points: Fibonacci sequence (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21). Relative estimation, not time-based.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 50×40 array of 4-byte elements occupies how much memory?
questions.solution: Size = rows×cols×bytes = 50×40×4 = 8000 B = 7.81 KB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop is a circuit that can store:
questions.solution: A flip-flop is a basic 1-bit memory element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol):
options: Resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses on a LAN
questions.solution: ARP: broadcast 'who has IP X?' → target replies with its MAC. Cache for efficiency.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key is:
options: A key in the same table
An attribute referencing a primary key in another table (maintains referential integrity)
questions.solution: Foreign key: ensures referential integrity (child table references parent).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Huffman coding algorithm uses:
questions.solution: Huffman: greedy (merge two smallest frequencies) using min-heap.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Chomsky hierarchy consists of:
questions.solution: Chomsky hierarchy: 0 (TM) ⊃ 1 (LBA) ⊃ 2 (PDA) ⊃ 3 (DFA/NFA).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Write allocate (fetch-on-write):
options: Writes directly to memory on miss
Loads the cache line on a write miss before writing
questions.solution: Write-allocate: good for write-back (subsequent writes hit). No-write-allocate: for write-through.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Non-uniform memory access (NUMA) means:
options: Access time depends on which processor accesses which memory region
questions.solution: NUMA: each CPU has local memory (fast) and remote memory (slower).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum bipartite matching can be found using:
questions.solution: Bipartite matching = network flow (source → left → right → sink, capacity 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 511 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 5 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Constant folding replaces:
options: Constant expressions with their computed values
questions.solution: Constant folding: 3 + 5 → 8 (compile-time evaluation).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Register allocation is typically solved using:
questions.solution: Register allocation = graph coloring (Chaitin's algorithm). Registers = colors, variables = nodes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An activity diagram is similar to:
options: A flowchart (shows control flow or workflow)
questions.solution: Activity diagram: actions, decisions, forks, joins (workflow modeling).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: Elements leave a queue in the same order they entered — FIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An NFA differs from a DFA because:
options: An NFA can have multiple transitions for the same input symbol
questions.solution: NFA: multiple possible next states (including ε-transitions). DFA: exactly one per symbol.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=500 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=500: O(500 log 500).
